title: Rate of wars
subtitle: The number of wars divided by the number of all states. This accounts for the changing number of states over time. Included are [interstate](#dod:interstate-war-mars) and [civil](#dod:civil-war-mars) wars that were ongoing that year.
caption:
Rate of wars

The number of wars divided by the number of all states. This accounts for the changing number of states over time. Included are interstate and civil wars that were ongoing that year.

This bar chart displays the global rate of wars from 1816 to 2011, defined as the number of wars divided by the number of all states. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 0.2, with grid lines marking intervals of 0.02. The data distinguishes between civil wars, represented by red bars, and interstate wars, represented by blue bars. Throughout the timeline, the rate of conflict fluctuates, with a distinct and massive spike occurring around 1918. During this peak, the rate of civil wars reaches the highest point on the chart, extending just above the 0.18 mark, while the rate of interstate wars simultaneously rises to approximately 0.125. Outside of this central peak, the rates generally remain lower. In the late 19th century, specifically around 1896, there is a smaller cluster where civil wars reach nearly 0.08 and interstate wars approach 0.06. Another period of elevated activity occurs between 1940 and 1950, where both war types show bars reaching between 0.04 and 0.06. From the 1960s through the 1990s, the bars are more frequent but typically stay below the 0.04 level, with civil wars often slightly higher than interstate wars. In the final years shown, from roughly 2000 to 2011, the bars become shorter and less frequent, with most values falling well below 0.02. The visual pattern highlights the 1918 period as a significant outlier compared to the rest of the recorded history.
